definition: Credit impulse conditioned on the Kalecki-Minsky taxonomy (fiscal-supported vs credit-supported regimes) is theoretically superior to unconditioned credit impulse for vol transition prediction. In Type A fiscal environments, credit deceleration is benign because the profit channel operates independently. Conditioned signal shows 3/3 hit rate vs 3/5 unconditioned.
evidence: Conditioned signal (credit impulse negative AND private sector deficit) shows 3/3 hit rate vs 3/5 unconditioned
Theoretical necessity: fiscal profit support and credit-driven leverage have different fragility profiles
Tested on only 4-5 US cycles with zero out-of-sample validation
2019 'hit' is potentially circular — COVID was exogenous, not Minsky-triggered
2-3 month Z.1 data lag limits real-time utility
open_questions: Is the 2019 hit circular given COVID was an exogenous shock?
Can the 2-3 month Z.1 data lag be overcome with higher-frequency proxies?
Has any out-of-sample or cross-country validation been performed?
Is this better described as a framework worth testing (6/10) rather than a validated predictor (3/10)?
